sentences.text: Lili Frank Grossman.
And my maiden name is Krakovics.
K-R-A-K-O-V-I-C-S.
F-R-A-N-K.
G-R-O-S-S-M-A-N.
I married the Dr.
[INAUDIBLE] Frank in Hungary in 1945.
And he died in 1983.
And three years later, I married David Grossman.
But I wanted to keep my family name before, the Frank, because everybody know me in Toronto Lili Frank.
That was the reason.
Jenu, Eugene in English.
Elisabeth Krakovics nee Landsman.
L-A-N-D-S-M-A-N.
1922, November 27.
Sajopalfalva, it's a little village in Hungary.
S-A-J-O-P-A-L-F-A-L-V-A.
Close to Miskolc, Borsod-Abauj-Zemplen.
It's 180 kilometer from Budapest, the capital of Hungary.
The Miskolc?
Sajopalfalva, it was a very small village.
Only 900 people there.
And we were the only-- only Jewish family in their village.
This time when I get school year, let's say-- I remember the best backward-- it was OK.
It wasn't any problem.
My father of was [? partu ?] with everybody there.
And my mother came from a bigger village, five kilometer away over there.
And it was a very simple.
We were hard-working people, my parents, I mean.
And we didn't have any special Jewish life.
We even to shul, synagogue, the next village.
And the next village was about three, four kilometer away from us.
And one more village came in that shul.
It was a little village shul.
Right.
What can I say?
When was-- when there was good, summer time, especially we went Saturday to shul.
Otherwise, we were going summertime to cheder.
Jewish-- Jewish school, we were learning, because I went five years in Catholic school in the village.
So we needed the summertime.
My brother and also from their other village we came where the synagogue was to the shochet place because we didn't have rabbi.
The shochet was the rabbi, the hazon, the cantor.
Orthodox.
Practicing Orthodox.
Yeah.
Yeah.
Everything was made at home.
We have people who have dark in the kitchen, one, two, three.
We have a special lady who-- who heated up the oven for bread baking.
It wasn't any problem.
Another lady who came to big washing because we have a well.
And we have to carry in their water and heat up on the stove.
And that was heating day.
And once a week, Thursday night, the whole family taking bath, also in the heated water.
But this came from the well.
It was very hard.
We have a butcher shop.
And we have a-- a [NON-ENGLISH], we--
Slaughterhouse.
We sold in wholesale the three, four, five villages butcher who came very small quantity meat to buy from us.
And we have a store, a grocery store.
But the grocery store means we sold dishes.
We sold yard of materials and even casket.
Even casket, because you have everything in the same village And it was an order store.
And-- and they have a [NON-ENGLISH].
They sold booze and wine and everything.
Don't--
No, they sold it.
People went and ordered it, wine and soda water and liquor and whiskey and everything.
That was a store, a non-Jewish one.
And we have a old maid teacher in the school.
And it was another teacher with families.
We were best friend together.
And this old maid has a brother in a bigger village.
And first time I met because of her anti-Semite.
Because we take the old maid teacher every Wednesday-- it was market day in the city-- and we took her to her brother's house.
He was a Greek pope--
Priest, yes, sorry.
Priest.
And she sent her little helper to our house, asking what time we are going tomorrow to the city.
But this afternoon in the school, she told me, told your father I am a friend of Hitler.
I didn't know who is Hitler.
Why should I know?
I was in grade three or four.

But some of the kids told their mother, and the mother, good friend of ours-- we were like together like that with everybody-- the mother run to my mother and asked, did you hear about the teacher said, Miss [?
Bodnar? ?] Did Lili said anything?
No.
And she told.
So when her little helper came to our house to asking, my father was a very [INAUDIBLE] character, good character.
And you know what she-- what he said?
He said, tell your teacher to go with Hitler to the city tomorrow morning.
It was a big, big things, you know.
We were-- I don't know if anybody slept this night at our house.
We were waiting for the gendarmes, you know.
I'm sure, he's coming to pick us up or my father or something.
But that wasn't anything.
And he-- she apologized.
She apologized.
The teacher, she apologized because she doesn't have any right to go.
But that was the first time when I heard Hitler's name.
That was the first time in my life.
Sure we went.
We all of us went.
It was market-- oh, yeah, it was as a market day.
And even I stayed Felsozsolca, a few kilometers from us.
And my mother was born over there.
So she came a smaller village by her marriage.
So sometimes I stayed with my grandfather.
And my mother has a step-mother.
But she was very nice.
And she liked, especially she was a cousin of my mother's real mother.
So she came from the family.
Sometimes I just stayed over there because I always got chocolate over there and things like that.
I want to ask you a little bit more about your family background.
You told me a little bit about your father, that he was a man of strong character.
What more can you tell me about him?
He was a very early orphan with five sister and brother.
When he was 15 years old, he was the bread winner.
He must be.
He has a mother full hand with kids, and two stores, small butcher shop and a grocery business.
And he then thought to buy up what they caught, you know, for the butcher shop.
And he was the bread-- bread winner.
He was the oldest son.
It was natural.
It wasn't any question.
Yeah.
Lots of brothers and sisters.
They were six.
They were six together.
And they died in-- partly in Auschwitz.
Partly they came home.
It was a miracle.
One of his-- his sister came home with a complete family.
That was a real miracle.
Yes, more or less.
But always in-- we can reach them.
It wasn't any question.
And in the next village was the cemetery.
And once a year, the whole family came together, everyone, small or big, everyone.
And we went together to the cemetery, to my father's parents and their parents came there.
Nowadays, we can't read those tombs.
You know?
The headstones.
They were buried in 1915 and 1918.
So it is over, like any other Jewish cemetery in Hungary.
Excuse me, and luckily the tombs is there because it was not marble.
Any marble tomb was stolen from Hungarian people.
Without any conscience, they stealing together because they can't carry that in a basket or in their pocket. six, seven people and wagon with horse ridden wagon.
But nobody knows anything.
And they making their new houses' steps from it.
I am wondering, and that's the reason I would like to go again, because my grandparents from my mother's side, they were well doing people, and they have two beautiful, tall, real marble tombs.
My great grandfather tombs was already stolen when I was there four years ago.
But I am wondering maybe I am afraid to know it was taken.
It is certain kind, hard cement, mixed something.
They were poor people, you know.
They didn't-- maybe this time they didn't come any marble from Italy.
So-- And all Jewish, everything gone.
So when I am going to visit my great-grandparents in my village, I mean, the next village, I'm just standing with one stone, because nobody, my brother and me, that's it.
Finished.
I'm just standing with one tombs and say the prayer because nobody visiting them anymore.
It's a very sad.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] So.
My mother's family was a very-- they were like called village, noble family, you know.
It's a good character, well-doing people.
And my grandfather was gabbai, which means president in the shul.
And my uncle was a very special man.
His schooling, or my mother's schooling, you know, she went in 1914, walking every single day five kilometer in the city and five kilometer to coming home because she went high school.
And that was--
Every single day, 10 months a year.
It wasn't a Jewish school.
When I became 14 years old, they kept me back, grade five in elementary school, because I was born end of November, and I should be 14 before-- 10 years old before September.
So I went five years.
So I went to Jewish school already, high school, to the city, walking also, or later on bike.
And more later it started the bus going to the city.
So that was the happy time already.
My brother.
My mother was one brother who was a very, very nice people.
He treated us whenever we met in the city.
And it was-- was a treat when he put was in a kosher, we called [NON-ENGLISH].
[INAUDIBLE] was the name of the salamis and hot dogs and everything.
And she always treated us.
He bought-- he, sorry-- he bought us the first skating, you know.
Yeah, the skates, yeah.
That's true my brother sold right away his.
And he bought some [INAUDIBLE] and sold with one or two forint, Hungarian money.
So he made a good deal.
He was interested about more to selling on the market than even in the school.
So he sold his skate.
And the he got another.
Lesley.
He's living in Toronto.
And I had one more brother, younger, 15 years old was in '44, 14 years old.
My own was 15.
Tibor.
Sajopalfalva.
My brother was born in 1921.
That's Lesley.
Tibor, when in the brick factory, June 11, when they sorted us out, and my father was sent to labor camp.
My mother, Tibor, and me, we went to the cattle wagons.
And we wanted Tibor to go with my father, men side.
He said, I am only 15 years old.
I don't want to be a soldier.
So he came with us.
He came he with us.
And June 13, when we arrived to Auschwitz, it was selected any way, men side and woman side.
And he went with my father's second cousin, an older man.
And we never, ever saw him again.
But when we were taken to the bath, shower, water where once a month, in Auschwitz from Birkenau, we always walked between the camps.
But it was sure fence all over.
So we were calling our relatives' name.
So we know he was in the gypsy lager.
The gypsy has a special lager.
And that gypsy lager, the first two weeks, all of the tent or something like that was evacuated.
That was terrible, because the gypsies was living over there long time.
And they knew what happens.
We were over there.
We smell the gas chamber, and we didn't know what was going on.
Nobody knows.
And when all Polish kapos who was living there four, five years, they said to us, keep quiet, because your parents is going up over there in the smoke.
Like they speak French, we don't know a-- a single word about it.
Nothing.
We just saw leave bushes, very tall, two lines.
And we saw a terrible chimney, tall, or two, I'm not sure.
And we-- we felt the smoke.
I told my mother, because we have grape yard.
And when we went to harvest the grapes, we roasted lambs.
And that was the smell over there.
Over there was lots of trees, [INAUDIBLE], like the Christmas trees, you know.
Evergreen.
And the Evergreen has a special movement.
It's always moving.
It doesn't need any wind.
And that took the smell--
Right and left and everywhere.
And I told mother-- my mother, always-- I feel the burning smell like at the harvest of the-- of the vineyard.
So we thought that this was a kitchen, you know.
So Dante's hell was heaven to describe--

He was taken to the gas chamber with the gypsies together because everyone over there beside the gypsies was between 14 and 16-years-old boys, mostly Hungarians, because this time it wasn't any more 14, 16 years old from Lithuania or Poland or anywhere.
It was all of them, 14 and 16-years-old young boys.
Sure, sure sorry.
From Sajopalfalva we went to live to Felsozsolca.
Yeah, because we became bigger.
And we didn't have any Jewish life.
And that was a dead end for us, the children.
So we went to the city anyway to schooling.
So we went to Felsozsolca.
It was 119 Jewish people there.
And the young people came together Saturday.
We have the shul.
We have also a shechot.
But time to time, one of the rabbi, real rabbi, came about 10 kilometers to see us and to giving us the line, how can we go further?
And went to school, to Miskolc.
What kind of a home did you have there in Felsozsolca?
Beautiful.
We built it.
We built that with two big stores, butcher and the grocery stores.
And that's true, we didn't have a pipe for water.
But we have a very small, little place, which we called bathroom.
It was only a so-called [NON-ENGLISH]--
Wash basin, yes, and we took the water in.
But we have a bathroom we could sit.
And I had one-- my own room.
And that was a big things.
The house was beautiful and was continuing with-- for the guy with family who take care of our farm.
And--
Yes.
He was the whole residence, yes.
And carts and horses and everything.
So it was a big.
It looked it was a rich, surrounding-- rich house that we had.
And we have good name.
Oh, yeah, we have a-- a woman in the kitchen who helped out everything.
And we have a lady who came for the washing day and the ironing day again.
But this time, we didn't made our own bread, because we have a bakery two houses from us.
So it was a big step for us.
It was almost called luxury.
He wasn't politically involved until 1944, April the 4th.
Because the German came in March, the 19.
I was in a movie in Miskolc with one or two boyfriends.
And all of the sudden, the movie closed, get dark.
And somebody came in and announced just the German--
Soldier just occupied Budapest.
And they are in the way to Miskolc and all over Hungary.
That was March 19.
So I-- and everything get dark.
And I was on my bike.
So I left the movie and the boys.
And I went home.
And my family was in the kitchen.
That was March.
It's cold time yet.
That was the family room.
And my father, [INAUDIBLE], was playing cards with friends.
And I said, don't play any more.
We are in big trouble.
The German occupying Hungary.
And one of the friend said, put together his hand, and said, Lilika, please do your lie?
I hope you lie.
It's not true.
Because we are going die, all of us.
It was terrible.
Everybody started to cry.
And everybody went home.
So then next day we were in Felsozolca. 4th of April, four gendarme came to our house.
Do not forget the gendarme was very good friend with us.
OK, I was 22 years almost, that-- 21 years old.
So came in, I was the so-called [NON-ENGLISH], which means a young lady.
And they came in.
They were joking.
And even then they came, for some reason they told us before we are-- we will be here 10 o'clock for officially to see the-- the scale and everything.
That was the gendarme's job.
The business scale in the butcher shop and the grocer.
But they told us before.
Yes, and they came in the kitchen.
And they have breakfast everything.
It was a good things to be good with them, you know, friendship.
And the four gendarme came.
And they said everybody stay seated with one gendarme.
We are going down in the basement because we know you are giving sign for the Russian airplanes from your home, from the attic.
